timeline.event: Born
Became a Langzhong (palace attendant) and traveled to regions such as Jiangnan, Shandong, and Henan while accompanying Emperor Wu
Before his father Sima Tan died of illness, he was asked to complete the compilation of the history book
Succeeded his father and became the Grand Astrologer (Taishiling)
Revised the calendar and completed the Taichu Calendar
Incurred the wrath of Emperor Wu while defending General Li Ling and was sentenced to castration
Died

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian)
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a 130-volume history book that chronicled the history from the Yellow Emperor, the first sovereign of China, to Emperor Wu, organized by prominent figures.

keyAchievements.title: Revision of the Taichu Calendar
keyAchievements.description: Revised the calendar in 104 BC and completed the Taichu Calendar.

faq.question: Who is Sima Qian?
faq.answer: A historian of the Former Han Dynasty in China, the author of the "Shiji" (Records of the Grand Historian), and considered one of the greatest historians of the East.

faq.question: Why was Sima Qian castrated?
faq.answer: In 99 BC, when General Li Ling was defeated by the Xiongnu, Sima Qian defended Li Ling, incurring the wrath of Emperor Wu and resulting in his castration.

faq.question: What are the major works of Sima Qian?
faq.answer: His representative work is the "Shiji" (Records of the Grand Historian), and other works include "Letter to Ren An" and "Lament for Not Meeting One's Time".

faq.question: What government positions did Sima Qian hold?
faq.answer: He succeeded his father as the Grand Astrologer (Taishiling), and after undergoing castration, he was appointed as the Palace Secretary (Zhongshuling).

timeline.event: Died

keyAchievements.title: Succession and Development of Daoist Thought
keyAchievements.description: Succeeded and developed Laozi's thought, becoming a representative figure of Daoism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy of Myriad Things Monism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated the monism of all things.

keyAchievements.title: Argument for Return to Nature and Nothingness
keyAchievements.description: Inheriting Laozi's thoughts, he argued for a return to nature and a return to nothingness.

keyAchievements.title: Pursuit of Individual Peace of Mind
keyAchievements.description: Unlike Laozi, he devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

keyAchievements.title: Presentation of a Philosophy for an Ideal Life
keyAchievements.description: He viewed the ideal life as one that discards the physical and mental roots of anxiety, reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility' to achieve freedom and independence.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Epistemology
keyAchievements.description: Established an epistemology that conceptual recognition and value judgments are meaningless, and that only thorough ignorance is correct.

faq.question: What was Zhuangzi's real name?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi's real name was Zhou (周).

faq.question: Whose thoughts did Zhuangzi succeed and develop?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi succeeded and developed the thoughts of Laozi.

faq.question: What government position did Zhuangzi hold?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi became an official of the lacquer tree garden in Meng.

faq.question: Did Zhuangzi accept the offer of King Wei of Chu?
faq.answer: King Wei of Chu requested him to assist in politics, but Zhuangzi refused.

faq.question: What is Zhuangzi's ideal life?
faq.answer: Zhuangzi viewed the ideal life as discarding the physical and mental roots of anxiety and reaching a state of mind of 'emptiness and tranquility'. Through this, he argued, one could follow the laws of nature and attain freedom and independence without being infringed upon by anything.

faq.question: How do Zhuangzi's thoughts differ from Laozi's?
faq.answer: While Laozi had some interest in the reality of politics and society, Zhuangzi devoted himself solely to individual peace of mind.

timeline.event: Born during the Tang Dynasty era.
In his hometown of Shu, he practiced Daoism while climbing the mountains of Sichuan with a hermit named Dongyanzi, and later left his hometown to travel around Jiangnan.
At the age of 43, he received an edict from Emperor Xuanzong and stayed in Chang'an as a Hanlin Gongfeng (academician).
Feeling bored with his life as an official, he argued with Gao Lishi, a close aide to Emperor Xuanzong, and resigned.
Returned to Jiangnan.
Joined the army of Prince Yong Lin, the sixteenth son of Emperor Xuanzong, but Prince Yong's army was designated as a rebel force, leading to his exile to Yelang (present-day Guizhou).
Died at the age of 61 at the home of his relative Li Yangbing, who was the magistrate of Dangtu County in Anhui Province.

keyAchievements.title: Great Poet in Chinese History
keyAchievements.description: A poet of the Tang Dynasty era, he is considered one of the greatest poets in Chinese history along with Du Fu.

keyAchievements.title: Poet Immortal (Shixian)
keyAchievements.description: Li Bai was called the 'Poet Immortal', and his poetic talent was evaluated as 'genius'.

keyAchievements.title: Prolific Poet
keyAchievements.description: Currently, about 1,100 of his poems survive, and his poetic literature themed around Daoism and wine is famous.

keyAchievements.title: Master of Gushi (Ancient Style Poetry) and Jueju (Quatrains)
keyAchievements.description: He specialized in gushi and jueju, and his jueju were evaluated as 'divine works'.

keyAchievements.title: Free and Fantastic Poetic Style
keyAchievements.description: His poems are grand in scale and full of vitality, sometimes featuring fantastic and extremely free characteristics.

faq.question: What were Li Bai's courtesy name and pseudonym?
faq.answer: Li Bai's courtesy name was Taibai, and his pseudonym was Qinglian Jushi (Householder of the Azure Lotus).

faq.question: By what nickname was Li Bai called?
faq.answer: Considered one of the greatest poets in Chinese history, Li Bai was called the 'Poet Immortal (Shixian)'.

faq.question: How many of Li Bai's poems survive?
faq.answer: Currently, about 1,100 of his poems survive.

faq.question: What are the main themes in Li Bai's poetry?
faq.answer: The main themes related to his poetic literature are Daoism and wine.

faq.question: Where was Li Bai from?
faq.answer: Currently, it is widely accepted that he was an Central Asian from Suyab of the Tang Dynasty.

faq.question: Did Li Bai receive formal education?
faq.answer: Because his father was a merchant, Li Bai did not receive formal education.

faq.question: When did Li Bai die?
faq.answer: Li Bai died in 762 at the age of 61 at the home of his relative Li Yangbing, who was the magistrate of Dangtu County in Anhui Province.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Li Bai's poetry?
faq.answer: His poems are grand in scale and full of vitality, sometimes featuring fantastic and extremely free characteristics.

timeline.event: Born as the son of a poor peasant in Nakamura, Aichi District, Owari Province (February 6, Tenbun 6, lunar calendar).
Began working as a servant for Oda Nobunaga.
Married Nene, the adopted daughter of Asano Nagakatsu (Eiroku 4).

Played an active role in the capture of Mitsukuri Castle in Omi, and when Oda Nobunaga installed Ashikaga Yoshiaki as Shogun, he managed political affairs in Kyoto alongside Akechi Mitsuhide and Niwa Nagahide (September, Eiroku 11, lunar calendar).
Joined the subjugation of Asakura Yoshikage of Echizen Province, and distinguished himself by commanding the rearguard at Kanegasaki (Genki 1).
After the annihilation of the Azai clan, he was granted three districts in Omi, took up his post as the lord of Nagahama Castle, and changed his surname to Hashiba (Tensho 1).
Returned without permission while being dispatched to rescue Shibata Katsuie, and subjugated Matsunaga Hisahide of Yamato Province (Battle of Shigisan Castle) (Tensho 4).
Subjugated Ukita Naoie, a daimyo of Bizen and Mimasaka Provinces (Tensho 7).
Attacked and received the surrender of Bessho Nagaharu, the lord of Miki Castle in Harima (Battle of Miki). Captured Arikoyama Castle of Yamana Akihiro of Tajima Province (Tensho 8).
Captured Tottori Castle (Battle of Tottori Castle). Placed Awaji Province under his control by capturing Iwaya Castle (Tensho 9).
Requested reinforcements from Oda Nobunaga during the water attack on Takamatsu Castle in Bitchu (Tensho 10).
Received the news that his lord Oda Nobunaga had committed suicide in the Honno-ji Incident (June 2, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Defeated Akechi Mitsuhide at the Battle of Yamazaki (June 13, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
At the Kiyosu Conference, Oda Nobunaga's legitimate grandson Sanboshi was decided as the successor (June 27, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Held Oda Nobunaga's funeral with his adopted son Hashiba Hidekatsu as the chief mourner (October 15, Tensho 10, lunar calendar).
Conflict with Shibata Katsuie intensified, leading him to besiege Nagahama Castle, capture Kajiki Castle, and receive Sanboshi from Oda Nobutaka (Tensho 11).
Defeated Shibata Katsuie at the Battle of Shizugatake (April 21, Tensho 11, lunar calendar).
Began the construction of Osaka Castle on the site of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji (Tensho 11).
Conflicted with Oda Nobukatsu, and Tokugawa Ieyasu sided with Nobukatsu (Battle of Komaki and Nagakute) (Tensho 12).
Ikeda Tsuneoki, his son Motosuke, and Mori Nagayoshi were killed in action at the Battle of Nagakute (April 9, Tensho 12, lunar calendar).
Made peace with Oda Nobukatsu (November 11, Tensho 12, lunar calendar).
Appointed to the court rank of Senior Second Rank and the office of Palace Minister (Naidaijin) (March 10, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Executed the conquest of Kishu (March 21, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Executed the conquest of Shikoku (July 25, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Ascended to the position of Kampaku (Imperial Regent) (July 11, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Sassa Narimasa of Etchu surrendered to Hideyoshi (August 25, Tensho 13, lunar calendar).
Forced his younger sister Asahi to divorce and married her off to Ieyasu, and sent his mother Omandokoro as a hostage to Ieyasu (Tensho 14).
Was granted the surname Toyotomi by the Imperial Court (September 9, Tensho 14, lunar calendar).
Ascended to the position of Daijo-daijin (Chancellor of the Realm) and established his regime (December 25, Tensho 14, lunar calendar).
Fought and was defeated by the Shimazu army led by Shimazu Iehisa at the Hetsugi River in Bungo (Battle of Hetsugigawa) (Tensho 14).
Led a large army of 200,000 to conquer Kyushu, resulting in the surrender of the Shimazu clan (Kyushu Campaign) (Tensho 15).
Issued the Bateren Edict expelling Christian missionaries (June, Tensho 15).
Implemented the Sword Hunt (Katanagari) (Tensho 16).
Tsurumatsu was born to his concubine Yodo-dono and was designated as his successor (Tensho 17).
Led a large army of 210,000 to dispatch troops to Kanto to suppress the Go-Hojo clan, and besieged Odawara Castle (Siege of Odawara) (Tensho 18).

After the death from illness of Tsurumatsu, whom he had designated as his successor, he adopted his nephew Hidetsugu, transferred the position of Kampaku to him, and was himself called Taiko (Tensho 19).
Ordered the tea master Sen no Rikyu to commit seppuku (Tensho 19).
Suppressed the rebellion led by Kunohe Masazane in the Tohoku region (Tensho 19).
The Imjin War (Japanese invasions of Korea) began (Bunroku 1).
Peace negotiations with Ming China began. His concubine Yodo-dono gave birth to Hideyori (Bunroku 2).
Disinherited Kampaku Toyotomi Hidetsugu, exiled him to Mount Koya, and later forced him to commit seppuku on charges of treason (Bunroku 4).
Peace negotiations with Ming China broke down (Bunroku 5).
Re-invaded Joseon Korea with an army of 140,000 (Jeongyu War) (Keicho 2).
Died at Fushimi Castle (August 18, Keicho 3, lunar calendar).

keyAchievements.title: Three Great Unifiers of the Sengoku Period
keyAchievements.description: Along with Oda Nobunaga and Tokugawa Ieyasu, he is known as one of the Three Great Unifiers of the Sengoku period.

keyAchievements.title: Succeeding Oda Nobunaga's Enterprise
keyAchievements.description: After the Honno-ji Incident, he defeated Akechi Mitsuhide and succeeded Oda Nobunaga's enterprise.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Japan
keyAchievements.description: Unified Japan by subduing the Go-Hojo clan and brought an end to the long-lasting Sengoku period.

keyAchievements.title: Appointment as Kampaku and Daijo-daijin
keyAchievements.description: Established his regime by being appointed Kampaku and Daijo-daijin, and was granted the surname Toyotomi by the Imperial Court.

keyAchievements.title: Taiko Land Survey and Sword Hunt
keyAchievements.description: Stabilized the domestic situation in Japan by implementing the Taiko Land Survey (Taikou Kenchi) and the Sword Hunt (Katanagari) policies.

keyAchievements.title: Rise from the Lowest Class to Ruler
keyAchievements.description: Ascended to become the ruler of Japan despite being a person from the lowest social class in the country.

keyAchievements.title: Construction of Osaka Castle
keyAchievements.description: Constructed Osaka Castle on the site of the Ishiyama Hongan-ji.

keyAchievements.title: Leading the Imjin War
keyAchievements.description: He was the main instigator who led the Imjin War in the first year of Bunroku (1592) and invaded Joseon Korea.

faq.question: What was Toyotomi Hideyoshi's background?
faq.answer: He is known to have been born as the son of a poor peasant in Nakamura, Aichi District, Owari Province. Because of his very low social status, he was conscious of his humble origins, leading to suspicions that records from his time and later periods were often distorted or glorified.

faq.question: Why couldn't Toyotomi Hideyoshi become a Shogun?
faq.answer: Coming from a commoner background and not being of the Minamoto bloodline, he could not become a Shogun due to the rule that 'only a Minamoto can become a Shogun'. Instead, he rose to the positions of Kampaku and Daijo-daijin.

faq.question: What were the main reasons Toyotomi Hideyoshi started the Imjin War?
faq.answer: General analysis suggests that he sought to acquire the justification to become Shogun by recovering the southern part of Joseon, which he claimed as former territory, and annihilating the Ming army. Furthermore, there is a theory that he intended to solidify the unstable vassal relationships by granting new territories to the samurai.

faq.question: What were Toyotomi Hideyoshi's physical characteristics?
faq.answer: He looked like a monkey and was called 'little monkey'. According to the records of the missionary Luis Frois, he was short, had an ugly face, and was polydactylous, having six fingers on his right hand.

faq.question: How was Toyotomi Hideyoshi's death handled?
faq.answer: He died at Fushimi Castle in the third year of Keicho (1598). Due to concerns about the army's morale, no funeral was held, and his death was kept a secret. His tomb was blown up and destroyed after the Battle of Sekigahara.

timeline.event: Born into a family of Ulama (Islamic scholars) in Tangier, Morocco.
Left his hometown at the age of 21 and embarked on the Hajj, a pilgrimage to Mecca.
Arrived in Alexandria.
After spending a month in Mecca, he joined a caravan returning to Iraq.
Arrived in Baghdad.
Left Baghdad and headed north along the Tigris River.
Arrived in Aden.
Visited Sultan Al-Hasan ibn Sulaiman of Kilwa.
Visited Mogadishu.
Decided to seek employment at the court of Muhammad bin Tughluq, the ruler of the Delhi Sultanate, and joined a caravan departing from Anatolia towards Delhi.
Arrived in Constantinople and had an audience with Andronikos III Palaiologos.
Arrived at the Samudera Pasai Sultanate located in the Aceh region of northern Sumatra.
Left Guangzhou and embarked on his journey back home.
Arrived in Damascus and heard the news that his father had died 15 years earlier.
When Alfonso XI of Castile attacked the Tangier region, he rallied with other Muslims to defend the port.
Left Fez and headed to Sijilmasa.
Arrived in Taghaza with a caravan.
Joined a caravan leaving Mali and headed to Timbuktu.
Returned to Sijilmasa.
Returned to Morocco.
Dictated his travelogue to Ibn Juzayy by the order of the Sultan, and Ibn Juzayy compiled and wrote the "Rihla of Ibn Battuta".
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Authoring the Famous Travelogue (Rihla)
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'Rihla', a famous travelogue that documented the medieval Islamic world and extensive regions beyond.

keyAchievements.title: Extensive Travels
keyAchievements.description: Traveled extensively over 120,000 km across contemporary Islamic countries, China, Sumatra, etc., leaving behind records of great cultural anthropological value.

keyAchievements.title: Appointment as Judge of the Delhi Sultanate
keyAchievements.description: Was appointed as a judge (Qadi) by Sultan Muhammad bin Tughluq of the Delhi Sultanate and served in that role.

faq.question: When and where was Ibn Battuta born?
faq.answer: Ibn Battuta was born on February 24, 1304, into a family of Ulama (Islamic scholars) in Tangier, Morocco.

faq.question: What value does Ibn Battuta's travelogue hold?
faq.answer: His travelogue describes an extensive journey of 120,000 km across almost all contemporary Islamic countries, China, and down to Sumatra, holding great cultural anthropological value.

faq.question: What professions did Ibn Battuta have?
faq.answer: He was a traveler, explorer, and author of the famous travelogue (Rihla). He also served as an appointed judge (Qadi) in the Delhi Sultanate and lived as a judge in Morocco during his later years.

faq.question: Who wrote Ibn Battuta's travelogue?
faq.answer: By the order of the Sultan, Ibn Battuta dictated his travelogue to Ibn Juzayy, who compiled and wrote the "Rihla of Ibn Battuta".
